title: "Ebony Throne Problem v19.21"

  description: "At 21:50, the Ebony Throne was discovered missing from a locked display in the western archives. Four individuals (1, 2, 3, 4) each had authorized entries near that time. Exactly one suspect lies in \emph{all} of their statements; the other three speak only truths. Precisely one is actually guilty. Each suspect provides five statements:

    Suspect 1:
      (1) 'If 2 was lying, then 3 could not have stolen the Throne.'
      (2) 'I left the archives at 21:40, so there's no way I took it at 21:50.'
      (3) '4 was definitely outside the western wing at 21:45.'
      (4) 'Either 2 never lingered beyond 21:35, or 3 made up a story—but not both.'
      (5) 'I saw 3 near the locked display at 21:45, unless 2’s timeline is false.'

    Suspect 2:
      (1) '1 actually remained until 21:55, contradicting that 21:40 claim.'
      (2) 'If 3 is telling the truth, then 4 must have stayed right until 21:50.'
      (3) 'I never touched that Ebony Throne after 21:30, so I'm not the thief.'
      (4) 'Either 1 is honest about leaving, or 3 is guilty—but not both.'
      (5) '4 was nowhere near that locked display, or 1’s timeline is false—only one is possible.'

    Suspect 3:
      (1) 'I was not anywhere close to the display after 21:35.'
      (2) 'If 4 was telling the truth, then 2's statement (3) is all wrong.'
      (3) '1 must have left early if 2 is lying—otherwise, I was definitely gone by 21:40.'
      (4) 'Either 2 never even approached the Throne, or 4’s logs are made up—but not both.'
      (5) 'I am not the thief; the logs show me in a separate hallway at 21:45.'

    Suspect 4:
      (1) 'If 1 is lying, then 2’s entire story must be correct.'
      (2) 'I have nothing to hide; I was in a different corridor at 21:50.'
      (3) 'Either 3 was near the display after 21:35, or 2 left around 21:40—but not both.'
      (4) '1 could not have left by 21:40, or 3’s statement (1) is false—only one of these is real.'
      (5) 'I am not guilty, so the culprit must be 1 or 2, never 3.'

  question: "Which suspect (1, 2, 3, or 4) stole the Ebony Throne? Provide only a single digit."

answer: >
  explanation: "Formal Predicate-Logic Approach:

  Let L(x) = 'x lies in all five statements,' T(x) = 'x is fully truthful,' and G(x) = 'x is guilty.'
  Exactly one suspect has L(x)=true, exactly one suspect is G(x)=true, the other three T(x).

  Brief Steps:

  1) Assume each suspect is the universal liar, check consistency with the others' statements as truths.
  2) Only one scenario remains contradiction-free, yielding the culprit.

  Case L(1):
    Then 1(1),(2),(3),(4),(5) are false => times or blame produce conflicts with 2(1) or 3(3), etc. Contradiction arises.

  Case L(2):
    Then 2(1)–(5) are false => (1) false => 1 did not stay 21:55 => maybe 21:40 stands => suspicious for 2. Meanwhile 1,3,4 are truthful => 1(2) 'left 21:40 => not thief' => G(1)=false, 3(5) 'I not thief => logs corridor' => G(3)=false, 4(5) 'not guilty => culprit is 1 or 2' => 1=not culprit => G(2). This scenario can hold with no contradiction.

  Case L(3):
    Then 3(1)–(5) false => 3 approached after 21:35 => suspicious. Meanwhile 1,2,4 are truthful => time or blame yields conflict with 1(5) or 4(3). Contradiction.

  Case L(4):
    Then 4(1)–(5) false => 1,2,3 are truthful => times produce direct mismatch with 2(5) or 3(4). Contradiction.

  Hence L(2)=> G(2) stands alone. Suspect 2 is the universal liar and the real thief.

  final: '2'"
